name: The Vierville Draw
premise: Three men with different functions and different views of the same catastrophe must coordinate across the chaos of Dog Green Sector to breach the wire, neutralize WN-71, and open the Vierville Draw before the rising tide makes the beach impassable and the second wave repeats the slaughter of the first.
goal: Breach the concertina wire obstacle at the base of the Vierville Draw using the Bangalore Torpedo, signal the USS McCook for direct fire support against WN-71, and lead survivors to the top of the bluffs by 0800 hours.
openingSituation: It is 0630 hours. The LCA is sixty yards from the beach and taking mortar near-misses. The Ranger sergeant is pinned behind a hedgehog in the surf. The medic is already in the water with casualties. The draw is defended, the wire is intact, and the tide is coming in.

beats.description: The coxswain must navigate the LCA through the obstacle belt — choosing between a sandbar risk and a mine-threaded gap — to land the men as close to dry sand as possible.
The Ranger sergeant, already in the water from an earlier craft, must reach the shingle without being cut down by the MG-42 at WN-71.
The medic encounters the first casualty wave in the surf and must make an immediate triage decision that will define his moral position for the rest of the session.
The three roles become aware of each other's presence and the shared problem: the wire at the draw's base is the bottleneck everything depends on.
Pinned at the shingle, the characters must gather information about the WN-71 firing arc — the key intelligence that will allow the USS McCook to engage the bunker effectively.

Lieutenant Hargrove attempts to organize a frontal assault on the wire; his plan is tactically sound but will cost every man in the open — the players must evaluate whether to follow, modify, or circumvent his orders.
Corporal Drennan, the demolitions man carrying the second Bangalore section, is wounded in the surf — the sergeant must decide whether to retrieve him or proceed with a partial charge.
The medic discovers that Private Olek Szymanski, the only man who has crawled close enough to observe the WN-71 Tobruk position, is categorized as expectant — but he is still conscious and talking.

The Bangalore Torpedo must be assembled and pushed through the concertina wire under direct fire — a physical and tactical problem requiring coordination between the sergeant and at least one other character.
Feldwebel Brandt's machine-gun position at WN-71 shifts its arc of fire, suggesting the crew has spotted the torpedo push — the characters must identify the new threat axis before detonation.

The coxswain, having beached the LCA and joined the survivors at the shingle, possesses navigational knowledge of the tidal schedule that changes the urgency calculus: the beach closes in forty minutes.
The USS McCook makes radio contact — but the fire mission requires a confirmed grid reference for WN-71 that only Szymanski's testimony can provide.
The Bangalore detonates — the wire is breached or it fails, and the characters must respond to whichever reality they have created.
The path through the draw is exposed; the survivors must move through it under fire from the remaining German positions while the USS McCook engages WN-71 directly.
The final choice: who goes up the bluff and who stays behind — the medic's triage logic now applies to the entire group.

At the top of the Vierville bluffs, the surviving characters look back at the beach as the second wave appears on the horizon — the draw is open, or it is not, and the cost of either outcome is visible in both directions.
